law: Expressing these features as intents allows people to use them through Siri, Spotlight search, and Shortcuts.
The sample app includes two key features that people are likely to use frequently: looking up information on a trail, and recording activity on a trail.
To make it easy for people to use these features without even opening the app, the sample code creates intents for them to use with Siri, Spotlight search, and Shortcuts.
An App Shortcut combines an intent with phrases people may use with Siri to perform the action, and additional metadata, such as an icon, and then uses this information in a Spotlight search.
To teach people a phrase to use the intent, the app provides a in the associated view.
The app doesn’t provide App Shortcuts for intents that people use less commonly.

This implementation provides a custom UI with a shorter supporting dialog to use when the custom UI is visible, and a different dialog containing additional information if the system can’t show the snippet.
An app intent can optionally require certain parameters to complete its action.
The sample app implements all of them for demonstration purposes, but a real app can use only the ones that meet its needs.
For example, the sample app focuses its UI on providing trail information, but people can also use its data to plan activities for a vacation.
The sample app uses a shared Swift package to organize its intents, entities, and data model so both the main app and a widget extension can use them.
Create custom shortcuts or use the provided “Find Closest” App Shortcut in Shortcuts.
Use custom shortcuts or the App Shortcut from Siri Suggestions.
This app intent opens a landmark in the app: To use your data as input and output of app intents and make the data available to the system, you use app entities.
It also adds required information to understand the data or to use it in system experiences.

For example, the of the sample app provides required and properties but doesn’t include every property of the model object: For more information about describing actions as app intents and app entities, refer to and .
The sample app’s conforms to the protocol: For entities that use different local and stable IDs, pairs them into a single ID: If a request stays on a device, the system uses the local ID.
The app describes its data as app entities so the system can use that data when running app intents.
Create app intents and entities so people can use your app’s content and actions across system experiences.
Use App Intents to define your app’s Focus filters, act on changes from the system, and filter notifications based on its parameters.
After launching the sample app, create a Focus filter for it by choosing Settings > Focus > Do Not Disturb (or another Focus) > Add Filter > Example Chat App.
Turn on the modified Focus to receive those parameters in the sample app’s App Intents extension.
It provides a default value for nonoptional parameters unless the filter requires the system to prompt for this value each time it enables it.
To use a custom object as a parameter, the sample project adds conformance to the object.

Use Siri to perform app actions like drafting and sending a message or reacting to a message using natural language.
This code snippet shows how the app’s conforms to the entity schema, including all properties that the schema requires.
People use Siri to interact with onscreen content.
The domain requires several intents to provide a consistent user experience.
The example also shows the implementation of the required union value type that the property uses: For more information about making actions discoverable by Apple Intelligence, see .
After choosing photos from their camera roll, people use Siri to perform actions like rotating a photo, adding it to their Favorites, and more.
To make sure the system can pass the sample app’s content to other apps, the app adopts the protocol for its app entities:
